section.title: Woe to Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: After sweeping the nations, the prophet turns the woe back on the holy city itself. This movement exposes Jerusalem's corrupt leaders and hardened heart, sets it against the perfect righteousness of the LORD who dwells within her, and ends with a call to wait for the God who will gather all nations for judgment. It is the book's sharpest indictment of the covenant people.
section.thesis: Jerusalem, the city of God, has become a city of oppressors whose leaders and prophets are corrupt, yet the LORD within her is righteous every morning; because she will not learn even from the ruin of the nations, He calls His people to wait for the day He rises to judge the whole earth.
section.frameNote: Two panels bound by contrast: the woe over the rebellious city and its corrupt leaders (1-4), set against the flawless righteousness of the LORD within her (5); then the lesson she refused to learn from the judged nations, ending in the summons to wait for the Day (6-8).

verses.text: {{hoy|Woe}} to the city of oppressors, rebellious and defiled!
She heeded no voice; she accepted no {{musar|correction}}. She does not trust in the LORD; she has not drawn near to her God.
Her princes are roaring lions; her judges are evening wolves, leaving nothing for the morning.
Her prophets are reckless, faithless men. Her priests profane the sanctuary; they do violence to the law.
The LORD within her is {{tsaddiq|righteous}}; He does no wrong. He applies His {{mishpat|justice}} morning by morning; He does not fail at dawn, yet the unjust know no shame.
“I have cut off the nations; their corner towers are destroyed. I have made their streets deserted with no one to pass through. Their cities are laid waste, with no man, no inhabitant.
I said, ‘Surely you will fear Me and accept correction.’ Then her dwelling place would not be cut off despite all for which I punished her. But they rose early to corrupt all their deeds.

Therefore wait for Me,” declares the LORD, “until the day I rise to testify. For My decision is to gather nations, to assemble kingdoms, to pour out upon them My indignation, all My burning anger. For all the earth will be consumed by the fire of My {{qinah|jealousy}}.

hoy.gloss: Woe, a cry of doom and lament often raised over the dead. The prophet who lifted this cry over pagan Philistia in chapter two now turns it on Jerusalem itself. That the holy city receives the same funeral wail as the nations is a shock: privilege has not exempted her; if anything, it deepens her guilt.

musar.gloss: Correction, discipline, instruction, the loving reproof by which a parent shapes a child. Jerusalem accepted no musar; she would not be taught, warned, or turned. Refusing correction is the mark of a hardened heart, and it is the very thing that seals her under judgment while the humble who receive it are spared.

tsaddiq.gloss: Righteous, just, in the right. Set against the corrupt princes, judges, prophets, and priests, the LORD within her is tsaddiq: He does no wrong at all. His flawless righteousness both condemns the city's corruption and holds out hope, for a righteous God can be trusted to set things right and to justify those who take refuge in Him.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of things. Every morning, without fail, the LORD brings His mishpat to light like the dawn, a steady, reliable justice that never sleeps. The tragedy is that the unjust feel no shame before it; God's perfect justice shines daily, yet the corrupt refuse to see or fear it.

qinah.gloss: Jealousy, zeal, burning ardor. The same fire of jealousy that closed chapter one returns to close this movement: all the earth will be consumed by it. God's jealousy is His holy, covenantal passion that will not tolerate rivals or injustice; it drives the gathering of the nations for judgment and clears the ground for the purified people of chapter three's second half.

basic.context: Having pronounced woe on the surrounding nations, Zephaniah aims the same word at Jerusalem. The city that should have been holy is called a city of oppressors: her officials are like roaring lions and hungry wolves, her prophets are reckless, her priests defile what is sacred. Against this dark picture stands one bright line: the LORD living in her midst is perfectly righteous, doing justice every single morning. Because the city refused to learn even from the ruin of other nations, God calls His people to wait for the day He rises to judge the whole earth.
basic.hermeneutics: The sharpest edge of this passage is that judgment begins with the house of God. Jerusalem's privilege, having the LORD in her midst, magnifies rather than excuses her sin. The contrast between corrupt leaders and the righteous God is meant to strip away religious pretense and drive the reader to fear the LORD, receive His correction, and long for the just world only He can bring.
basic.originalLanguages: The funeral cry hoy, woe, once aimed at pagans, now falls on Jerusalem. Her fatal flaw is that she accepted no musar, no correction. Against her corruption the LORD stands as tsaddiq, righteous, bringing His mishpat, justice, morning by morning without fail. And the movement ends where chapter one ended, in the fire of God's qinah, His jealous, all-consuming zeal.
basic.typology: The righteous LORD within the city, doing justice every morning while the corrupt know no shame, points to Christ, who came into the corrupt heart of Jerusalem as the truly Righteous One, and to His return as the Judge who will gather all nations. The failed shepherds, princes, prophets, and priests point by contrast to the true Shepherd, Prophet, Priest, and King.
basic.christ: Jesus is the LORD within her, the Righteous One who walked Jerusalem's streets doing no wrong, exposing her corrupt leaders and weeping over the city that would not receive correction. Where her princes devoured and her priests profaned the sanctuary, Christ is the good Shepherd who lays down His life and the great High Priest who cleanses the temple. He bore the fire of God's jealousy so that those who fear Him and receive His correction escape the Day He will bring.
basic.god: We learn that God is perfectly righteous, tirelessly just, and present with His people, and that His presence intensifies their accountability. He is a God who disciplines in love, longing that His people fear Him and accept correction so they need not be cut off. And He is patient, waiting, yet resolved: He will finally rise to judge all the earth, for His justice never fails even when human justice is utterly corrupt.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 22 gives the same portrait of Jerusalem's corrupt princes, priests, and prophets. Luke 19 shows Christ weeping over the city that would not know the day of its visitation. And 1 Peter 4 declares that judgment begins with the household of God.
basic.oneStory: This movement lays bare the failure of God's own city and its leaders, and sets against it the flawless righteousness of the LORD who dwells within her. That righteous presence is realized in Christ, who came into Jerusalem, bore its judgment, and will return to gather the nations, clearing the way for the purified, rejoicing people the book's final movement promises.

context.literary: The movement is built on a stark contrast. Verses 1-4 pile up the city's sins and the corruption of her four leadership classes: officials, judges, prophets, and priests. Verse 5 stands as the hinge, a single luminous line about the righteous LORD in her midst, mirroring the corruption point for point. Verses 6-8 then recount God's unheeded object lesson in the judged nations and close with the summons to wait for the Day, echoing the fire-of-jealousy language of 1:18 and binding the book's judgment sections together.
context.historical: The oppression, violence, and false prophecy described fit Jerusalem in the decades before its fall in 586 BC. The leaders named, princes, judges, prophets, and priests, were the very classes charged with justice and true worship, now turned predator and profaner. The reference to nations already cut off, with ruined towers and deserted streets, likely recalls recent Assyrian devastations that Judah witnessed but failed to learn from.
context.canonical: The indictment echoes Isaiah 1, Jeremiah, Micah 3, and Ezekiel 22, where corrupt leadership provokes judgment on Jerusalem. The theme that the LORD is righteous while His people are not runs to Paul's declaration that God is just and the justifier of those who have faith in Jesus, and to Peter's warning that judgment begins with God's household.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose Jerusalem's rebellion and the corruption of her leaders, to magnify by contrast the unfailing righteousness of the LORD who dwells among her, and to summon God's people to fear Him, receive correction, and wait for the certain day of His just reckoning with all the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the city's sins and God's coming judgment; the corruption of her leaders is exposed, not endorsed. The prescriptive notes are to fear the LORD and accept His correction, verse 7, and to wait for Me, verse 8, trusting God's justice rather than despairing or taking matters into our own hands.

debates.question: What does it mean to wait for Me until the day I rise (verse 8)?

views.label: A call to the faithful remnant to trust God's timing
views.case: Amid pervasive corruption, the humble are to wait in hope for the LORD to act justly rather than lose heart or seek their own vengeance, confident that His justice, though delayed, is certain.

views.label: A sober warning that judgment is fixed
views.case: The waiting underscores that the Day is coming without fail; the verb rise pictures God taking the stand as witness and judge, so the summons presses everyone to reckon with an unavoidable reckoning.

debates.bearing: Both readings meet in the same posture: because God's justice is certain, His people wait on Him in trust, and the complacent are warned that delay is not the same as escape.

debates.question: Why does the passage stress that the LORD within her is righteous (verse 5)?

views.label: To condemn the city by contrast
views.case: The flawless justice of God, delivered fresh every morning, exposes the depth of the leaders' corruption; they sin in the very presence of perfect righteousness and feel no shame.

views.label: To ground hope for the remnant
views.case: That God Himself is righteous and present means the city's corruption is not the last word; a just God can be trusted to set things right and to preserve a people for Himself.

debates.bearing: The two work together: the same righteousness that condemns the unjust who know no shame is the hope of the humble, for a righteous God both judges evil and justifies those who trust Him.

study.originalLanguages: The movement opens with hoy, woe, the funeral cry the prophet had aimed at pagan nations, now shockingly turned on the holy city. Jerusalem's root failure is stated with a single word: she accepted no musar, no correction or discipline, the loving reproof that shapes a child; she would not be taught or turned. Against her stands the LORD, described as tsaddiq, righteous, who does no wrong and who brings His mishpat, justice, la-boqer ba-boqer, morning by morning, as reliably as the dawn, and yet the unjust know no shame before this daily blaze of righteousness. The movement closes as chapter one did, in the fire of God's qinah, His jealous, consuming zeal that will gather the nations and purge the earth, binding the book's judgment together and clearing the ground for the promised remnant.

typology.type: The righteous LORD dwelling within the corrupt city
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Righteous One, entering Jerusalem, exposing its sin and weeping over it, Luke 19:41-44 and 1 John 2:1.

typology.type: The corrupt shepherds, princes, prophets, and priests
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the true Shepherd, Prophet, Priest, and King who does no violence to the law but fulfills it, John 10:11 and Hebrews 7:26.

typology.type: God rising to gather the nations for judgment
typology.fulfillment: Christ returning to judge the living and the dead and gather all peoples before His throne, Matthew 25:31-32.

study.christ: Zephaniah says the LORD within her is righteous, doing no wrong; centuries later that righteous LORD walked Jerusalem's streets in the flesh. Jesus is the Righteous One who exposed the roaring-lion officials and the sanctuary-profaning priests, who wept over the city that would not accept correction or know the day of its visitation. Where her leaders devoured the flock, He is the good Shepherd who gives His life for the sheep; where her priests did violence to the law, He is the High Priest who fulfills it and cleanses the temple. He absorbed the fire of God's jealousy at the cross, so that all who fear the LORD and receive His correction are spared the Day when He rises to judge the earth.
study.god: The LORD of this passage is stunningly righteous: He does no wrong, and He dispenses justice every morning without fail, as dependable as the sunrise. His presence in the midst of His people is both their glory and their greater accountability, for they sin in the light of perfect righteousness. He is a disciplining Father who sends correction in love, grieved when it is refused, and patient in delaying judgment, yet utterly resolved to rise at last and set the whole world right. His justice never sleeps even when human justice is wholly corrupt.

crossReferences.note: The same fourfold indictment of Jerusalem's princes, priests, prophets, and people, devouring and profaning.
Christ weeps over Jerusalem, the city that did not accept correction or know the time of its visitation.
Judgment begins with the household of God, the principle behind woe falling first on the holy city.
God is just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus, the hope grounded in the righteous LORD within her.

study.oneStory: The woe over Jerusalem shows that even God's chosen city, with corrupt shepherds and a hardened heart, stands condemned before the righteous LORD in her midst. That contrast drives the whole story toward Christ, the Righteous One who came into the city, bore its judgment, and will return to gather the nations. The refusal of correction that dooms Jerusalem is the backdrop for the grace of the next movement, where God Himself creates a humble, honest, rejoicing remnant, doing for His people what they could never do for themselves.

sermon.title: The Danger of Spiritual Paralysis
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly on Zephaniah 3:1-5, the heart of this movement, treating the corruption of the rebellious city and its leaders against the righteousness of the LORD in her midst. A clear, orthodox exposition of the passage, confirmed on the Truth For Life archive this session as a Zephaniah 3:1-5 sermon.

quiz.q: Whom does the woe fall on in this movement?

options.text: Jerusalem itself, the city of oppressors, rebellious and defiled.
options.note: Zephaniah 3:1 turns the prophetic woe from the nations onto the holy city.

options.text: Only the surrounding pagan nations.
options.note: The nations received woe in chapter two, but here the same cry turns onto Jerusalem itself.

options.text: The Babylonians who will destroy the city.
options.note: The woe is aimed at Jerusalem's own sin, not at her future invaders.

options.text: The prophet Zephaniah himself.
options.note: The woe is against the corrupt city and its leaders, not the prophet.

options.text: Princes like roaring lions, judges like evening wolves, reckless prophets, and priests who profane the sanctuary.
options.note: Zephaniah 3:3-4 indicts all four leadership classes as corrupt predators and profaners.

options.text: Faithful shepherds who guard the people.
options.note: The opposite is true; they devour and profane rather than protect.

options.text: Weak but well-meaning men doing their best.
options.note: Nearer in sensing failure, but the text names active corruption and violence, not mere weakness.

options.text: Foreigners who had seized power in the city.
options.note: These are Jerusalem's own officials, judges, prophets, and priests.

quiz.q: What single bright contrast stands against the city's corruption?

options.text: The LORD within her is righteous, doing justice morning by morning without fail.
options.note: Zephaniah 3:5 sets God's flawless daily justice against the leaders' corruption.

options.text: A few honest judges still held out in the city.
options.note: Closer, since it looks for righteousness in the city, but the text points not to good officials but to the righteous LORD Himself.

options.text: The temple building remained magnificent.
options.note: The priests were profaning the sanctuary; the contrast is God's own righteousness, not the building.

options.text: The surrounding nations had repented.
options.note: The contrast is the righteous LORD in her midst, not the nations.

quiz.q: Why is Jerusalem's refusal of correction so serious (3:2, 7)?

options.text: Refusing correction reveals a hardened heart that will not fear God or turn, sealing her under judgment.
options.note: She accepted no correction, 3:2, and corrupted her deeds even after warning, 3:7.

options.text: It merely showed she was independent-minded.
options.note: The text treats it as hardened rebellion, not healthy independence.

options.text: It was a minor failing compared to her idolatry.
options.note: It sits alongside her idolatry, but refusing correction is central here, the root that keeps her from repenting.

options.text: It had no real bearing on her judgment.
options.note: Her refusal of correction is precisely why judgment stands, unlike the humble who receive it.

quiz.q: How does the righteous LORD in her midst point to Christ?

options.text: Jesus is the Righteous One who came into Jerusalem, exposed its sin, and bore its judgment.
options.note: Christ, the LORD within her in the flesh, wept over the city and gave His life, Luke 19:41.

options.text: It shows God stayed distant from the corrupt city.
options.note: The point is the opposite: the righteous LORD dwells in her midst, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It points to the temple as the holy place in her midst.
options.note: The temple did symbolize God's presence, but the righteousness in her midst is God Himself, made flesh in Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to Jesus.
options.note: The righteous LORD within her is fulfilled precisely in Christ among His people.

questions: Jerusalem accepted no correction and would not be taught. How do you respond when God, through Scripture or others, brings correction into your life?
The leaders meant to protect the people instead devoured them. Where have you seen trusted authority abused, and how does God's perfect justice comfort you there?
Every morning the LORD brings His justice to light without fail. How does the reliability of God's righteousness steady you when the world around you seems unjust?
God said, surely you will fear Me, longing that His people would turn. Where is He patiently calling you to fear Him and change before the pattern hardens?
The righteous LORD in the midst of the city is fulfilled in Jesus, who wept over it and bore its judgment. How does His righteous presence with you shape the way you live today?

translationNotes.note: City of oppressors renders a Hebrew phrase; the BSB captures the sense of a rebellious, defiled, and oppressing city, the same word later prophets use of Jerusalem's violence.
Evening wolves leaving nothing for the morning pictures judges so rapacious they devour everything by nightfall, leaving no scraps, an image of insatiable injustice.
Morning by morning renders the doubled Hebrew la-boqer ba-boqer, stressing the unfailing, daily regularity of God's justice, like the dawn that never fails to come.
I rise to testify follows the LXX and Syriac; the BSB footnote notes the Hebrew reads rise up to plunder. Either way God rises to act decisively on the Day of judgment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zephaniah 3:1-8. All 8 verses reproduced contiguously. No em dashes occurred in this passage. Sermon by Alistair Begg on Zephaniah 3:1-5 verified on the Truth For Life archive this session.
